text: The surgeon begins dissection from the sigmoid colon, moving downward towards the center. The bladder is visible and adherent to the bowel, while the uterus remains obscured by adhesions. The surgeon palpates the area to identify structures under the adhesions.

The surgeon makes bold cuts on the bladder using laparoscopic shearers, while identifying and lateralizing the ureter. The infundibulopelvic ligament and gonadal vein are visible, serving as important landmarks in the distorted anatomy. The suction cannula is used as a blunt dissector to gently separate the infundibulopelvic vein from the pararectal space, creating more space for deeper dissection.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate parasigmoidal fat from ovarian adhesions, revealing endometriotic ovarian tissue on the left side. The sigmoid colon is plastered to the bladder, and the appendix is adherent to both the ovary and sigmoid colon. A chocolate cyst is decompressed, creating more space in the pelvic plane. Energy sources are recommended for dissection due to uncertain tissue planes.

The surgeon identifies dense adhesions and suture material, likely proline, on the ovarian tissue. The appendix is used as a guide to locate the ovary, which then guides the dissection towards the sigmoid colon. The bladder is partially identified, and a bold incision is made over it, revealing the suspected uterus beneath. The round ligament is identified and used as a guide for further dissection, opening spaces below it into the paravesical space. The uterine origin and ureter are visible despite the dense adhesions.

The surgeon performs further dissection to separate the bladder from the uterus, revealing the fundus of the uterus severely affected by endometriosis. Bold cuts are made over the dome of the bladder, releasing chocolate-colored fluid from the endometriotic tissue. The round ligament aids in separating the bladder, providing a clear view of the cervix and vagina for anterior dissection. The ureter is carefully lateralized away from the fibrosed pararectal plane, and the ovarian remnant is identified.

The surgeon identifies peritoneal tissue near the ovary to facilitate dissection into the pararectal space, aiming to mobilize the rectum and sigmoid colon laterally. The appendix is lifted to reveal the ureter, allowing further lateral dissection. Dense adhesions are dissected using a shearer, with visible suture material indicating previous surgical intervention. The ureter serves as a landmark for separating the deep pararectal space, enabling central bowel dissection.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to make bold cuts, mobilizing the upper part of the rectum adherent to the posterior aspect of the uterus. The dissection progresses to the level of the utero-sacral ligaments. A nodule at the posterior cervix is identified and dissected using traction, counter-traction, and electrocoagulation. The uterus is removed, and the surgeon enters the Denonvilliers' fascia, separating the nodule from the anterior rectum, freeing the bowel and opening the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon uses bold cuts to separate fibrotic adhesions between the pararectal part and the posterior aspect of the cervix. Traction and counter traction are applied using a suction cannula to liberate the remaining point adhesion, allowing the adherent bowel to be lifted out of the pelvis. The hysterectomy proceeds with colpotomy using a laparoscopic shearer, and the uterine specimen is extracted vaginally. A small vaginal nodule is identified and liberated during the colpotomy. A drain is placed inside the abdomen due to intensive dissection at the bowel level.

The patient is currently two weeks following surgery and has recovered absolutely perfectly following the surgery. Bowel testing is done with methylene blue. Bladder testing is done with methylene blue plier to crochet. We have not devascularized the ureter enough that we would consider to stent it. As you can see appendicectomy done which would mark the end of the surgical procedure for this patient.
